universities.restricted_areas:
Pharmacy, Nursing, Education*, Computer Science, Computer Engineering, Accounting, and students needing a full courseload in Psychology are not able to take coursework at USF.  Biology, Finance, Math, Chemistry, Supply Chain, Economics, and all Engineering majors have strict prerequisites and additional review to confirm course requirements are met prior to course approval. Advising staff in these areas to review your transcripts and course syllabi prior to issuing registration permissions and approval is not guaranteed.  

Home institution course approval does not guarantee course approval at USF as the University has a strict state mandated pre-requisite format for coursework.  Transcripts must be evaluated to confirm requirements are met for requested classes. 
Exchange students may need additional permissions to register even if they do not see a prerequisite. Coursework is also restricted by student standing, for example, it may be open to only upper classmen or Junior/Senior standing – this means it is open to third- and fourth-year students. Registration permissions will need to be obtained from the department for these classes. 
Exchange students attending USF for two semesters submit course requests both semesters to the inbound exchange coordinator so they can facilitate course approval as appropriate. 
 
Exchange students may not take Internship and/or thesis classes in their first semester of exchange
 
Students who are wanting to take an elective in the College of the Arts, must wait until every end of the semester to register as classes are restricted until late December for Spring and mid to late August for Fall. 

* While the majority of courses in the College of Education are unavailable, there are a select few offered each semester that are available but not guaranteed. Incoming exchange students may not register in practicum, classroom placement, or other courses tied to education licensure.  Please contact the incoming exchange advisor for more information.

All UCU courses are open to exchange students. However, some courses are highly competitive. Therefore, students need to be flexible in choosing courses. Students need to indicate 8-10 preferred courses (4 first choice and 4-6 alternative choices) per semester in the UCU's online platform (OSIRIS). Students are allowed a maximum of 2 courses in the same track. A standard course load is 4 courses (7,5 ECTS per course) per semester. Students should fulfill their mandatory graduation requirements at their home university in case they are not placed in their first choice courses. Exchange students can only take courses offered by UCU (and not by other Utrecht University faculties).

universities.admission_notes:
Module selection: https://www.exeter.ac.uk/study/studyabroadprogrammes/moduleselection/

* Study Abroad and International Exchange students can usually (if the agreement with your home university allows) choose modules from across our departments (excluding the College of Medicine and Health, Liberal Arts, and the Graduate School of Education) and at any undergraduate level. However, please note the following:
- You must meet any pre-requisites stated in the module description
- You must choose a level suitable for your prior knowledge of the subject area
- Choosing modules from across different departments and levels will increase the possibility of timetable clashes. This can make finalising your study programme more difficult. Therefore you should choose a minimum of 50% of your modules in one subject area.
- We are unable to guarantee that the modules you initially choose will be available. Your study programme will be finalised in the first two weeks of your arrival at the University of Exeter.

Both Fall and Spring

universities.restricted_areas:
there are no new restrictions at this time. However, as a reminder, here are our current restrictions:

Our study abroad programme is designed for undergraduate students. Most students will only be able to take undergraduate units (Years 1–3). A few schools offer M-level units, but Master’s students should only be nominated if they are able to take UG units as part of their study abroad programme.

Psychological Science:
This subject is taught as an experimental science at Bristol, not as a social science. Students should have a strong background in Chemistry, Biology, and Neuroscience. The school recommends students take no more than one third-year unit per semester, and second-year unit availability is limited.

Engineering:
Unit availability varies by programme, and single-semester students should be flexible with their choices. 

Mechanical Engineering: Limited 1st and 2nd year units.

Civil Engineering: Few units available for single semester students. Usually full-year study is recommended.

Aerospace Engineering: Limited lower-year units.

Electrical & Electronic Engineering: Very few units across all years, particularly for single semester students.

Our updated factsheet for 2026/27 will be shared with partners in January 2026.

[Yonsei students are guaranteed placement within their top three choices, provided they meet the academic requirements outlined in the “Academics” section on the program page. Please refero to the 'Restricted study area']
1. Visit Program Finder: https://search.isepstudyabroad.org/FindAProgram/Map 
2. Type 'Yonsei University' in the pop-up menu to start. 
3. Select the semester, year, region, and language you are applying for. **Non-English program applicants(비영어권 지원자) must select the language they took their language proficiency test for. Click 'More filters' and select 'ISEP exchange' under Program section. 
4. The results will be narrowed down. Once you click a university of your interest among the search results, you will be directed to its profile page. Scroll down to the bottom and select its program (it must be ISEP Exchange). The details on the program can be found there. The available areas of study may differ by university.
universities.restricted_areas:

universities.admission_notes:
<Language Requirements>
https://www.isepstudyabroad.org/planning/how-to-apply/english-language-requirements
• Language requirements vary by program (university) and can be found on each program page.  In certain countries, such as Spain, additional proof of language proficiency may be necessary (either a test certificate issued within the past 2 years or an assessment from the home university faculty) in the country's first language, even if the student opts for an English-language program.
• Students must have the required documents prepared when applying. Therefore, students are strongly advised to conduct thorough research on the specific requirements of their preferred universities before submitting their applications. Notably, some UK universities may not accept TOEFL scores, so students applying to these institutions should ensure they possess a valid IELTS score along with the TOEFL score at the time of application. It is important to note that obtaining an IELTS score after the application deadline is not feasible.

universities.english_notes:
<Language Requirements>
• Our educational system - Problem Based Learning (PBL) - requires you to actively take part in the tutorial meetings. The PBL method is explicitly student-centred rather than teacher-centred, and the students are expected to take the initiative. Speaking up and actively taking part in discussions is a crucial part of the system and this will also influence your final grades. Therefore, it is extremely important that you have an excellent knowledge of both written and spoken English.

Master’s degree programs
PhD program
Special classes for exchange students
Modules
Study & Exam regulations
Course catalogue

Enrollment in the following courses is subject to availability of lab classes and to prior approval by the institute/department: biochemistry, bioinformatics, and biology. Restrictions apply in pharmacy, law, business administration, and economics.
The Law Department prefers direct exchange students from institutions who also accept law students. There are only very few places available for students from other exchange programs.
The School of Business and Economics requires excellent German language skills (B2 level or above) for classes taught in German. There is a very limited number of classes in English, and they are not always offered regularly.
Please note that Human Medicine and Veterinary Medicine are NOT open to exchange students.
Freie Universität Berlin offers a range number of master’s degree programs. The following programs are open for postgraduate exchange students without restriction: modern German literature, philosophy, and political science.
All other master’s degree programs are restricted and need prior approval from the institute/department.

universities.available_areas:
* Faculty of Economics and Business Administration

* Students must choose 1 campus among Campus Brussels/Leuven/Antwerp. 
- Antwerp campus has management and business courses on bachelor & Master level. Courses on the bachelor level only available in the first semester (for example: finance, accounting and harbour logistics). Groups tend to be small and teach hands-on.
- Brussels campus has a strong focus on business administration and management (both bachelor and master entirely in English with additional minors).
- Leuven campus has a smaller offer for bachelor students but has a large offer for master students with many interesting courses in economics, business economics and business engineering. Students who want to study on the Leuven campus need to be strong in Mathematics & statistics as courses tend to be very analytical. 

Students are allowed to take maximum 6 ECTS at another campus then their main campus
universities.restricted_areas: Students selected at the faculty of Economics & Business, need to take the majority of the courses at this faculty, however they are allowed to take maximum 2 courses from another faculty, but only after approval from the student advisor at the faculty of Economics & Business who'll contact the coordinator at the other faculty.
universities.admission_notes:

- Photo

- Official transcript of records of all previous years at the university
- learning agreement

We don't request GPA from exchange students.

https://www.ugent.be/prospect/en/administration/application/languagerequirements.htm
 - Faculty of Bioscience Engineering: TOEFL IBT 80
 - Faculty of Engineering and Architecture / Faculty of Sciences / Faculty of Psychology and Educational Sciences: TOEFL IBT 87
 - Faculty of Economics and Business Administration: TOEFL IBT 90

<Semester Fulfillment Requirement>
* Master courses: Available to bachelor students only if the student have completed their third year before starting the exchange semester.
* Economics and Business Administration courses: Study level Bachelor: courses are instructed at 3rd year Bachelor. This means that prospect students need to have completed minimum 120 ECTS in the (relevant) home degree by the start of the exchange period.
Study level Master: to include Master courses in the curriculum, prospect students need to have completed minimum 180 ECTS in the (relevant) home degree by the start of the exchange period. Students need to check if they meet the initial competences of the course requirements.

Exchange studetns are also eligible to apply to undertake the Australian National Interships Program (ANIP) and the College fo Bsuiness and Economics (CBE) Special Industry Project (SIP). Separate application deadlines apply. Contact global.programs@anu.edu.au for more infromation. 

ANIP - https://anip.anu.edu.au/
SIP - https://cbe.anu.edu.au/current-students/opportunities-and-careers/special-industry-project-course

universities.admission_notes:
1) Applicants must have completed at least one academic year of undergraduate studies and must be a registered undergraduate in their home university throughout the study term
2) Students from non-English-speaking countries or from institutions whereby the language of instruction is not English, must fulfil at least one of the listed English language proficiency certifications.:

1)	TOEFL PBT: Min. of 583 (with min. 5.5 for writing & 50 for reading) OR
2)	TOEFL iBT: Min of 93 (with min. 22 for reading & 22 for writing) OR
3)	IELTS: Min. of 7.0 (with min. 7.0 for reading & 6.5 for writing) OR
4)	C1 Advanced: Min. of 185 (with min. of 185 for reading and 176 for writing)

Incoming exchange students are only permitted to take courses through the College of Literature, Science, and the Arts(LSA); https://lsa.umich.edu/lsa/academics/courses.html
Incoming exchange students are not permitted to take courses in Electrical Engineering and Computer Science (EECS). 

Incoming exchange students are encouraged to stay in UM on-campus housing. Unfortunately, UM on-campus housing is not guaranteed and it is extremely difficult for exchange students to secure a spot in on-campus housing. Interested students should apply for UM on-campus housing, but should also look for off-campus housing options in the event that they cannot secure on-campus housing. 

Only undergraduate students may apply for the exchange program.
universities.restricted_areas:
In general, exchange students should plan on selecting undergraduate coursework from the College of Literature, Science, and Arts (LSA). Access to courses in other units is severely limited and requires special permission and overrides, which may not be approved.
 Qualified students apply to the College of Literature, Science, and the Arts and may enroll in courses from any department in the college. Courses in Economics, Computer Science, Communication Studies, Psychology, and Political Science are particularly difficult to enroll in. CGIS cannot guarantee enrollment in any specific courses, especially from these four departments. Therefore, students should not depend on enrollment in courses primarily from these departments.

universities.available_areas:
As an exchange student, you can take a wide array of courses. However, you cannot register for more than two finance classes or two economics courses (see note below) or any graduate level (MBA) courses, and may only take one non-Stern (i.e. non-business) class. This non-business class can be taken from the College of Arts and Science (CAS). Exceptions to the one non-Stern class rule will only be made for Economics classes. Our Economics courses are taught between Stern and the College of Arts and Science (CAS), and you may take a maximum of two Economics classes while on exchange. Your Economics classes can either be both at Stern, both at CAS or one from Stern and one from CAS. If you select two Economics classes from CAS, the remainder of your courses must be taken at Stern.

You can use the Albert Course Search to view course offerings by semester. Course descriptions are available in the Stern Undergraduate Bulletin, the Stern Course Index, and the College of Arts and Science (CAS) Bulletin.

universities.restricted_areas:
Academic Restrictions
Students should have significant academic preparation in the discipline of their chosen major/program at Cornell. Students will take at least 50% of their courses in the department or college that is hosting them. Other courses may come from across the college or university.
Undergraduate students may apply to any undergraduate college covered by the exchange agreement. Generally, undergraduate exchange students are not approved to take graduate-level courses.
 
 Exchange students will be permitted to enroll in any course for which they are qualified, subject to meeting admission requirements, prerequisites, and timetable and capacity constraints at Cornell. However, there are specific academic restrictions to bear in mind: 
 
 Exchange students are unable to take courses in Computer Science (CS). Related subjects may be found in other departments (Electrical and Computer Engineering; Information Science, Systems, and Technology; or Operations Research and Information Engineering).
 Only students in business degree programs can apply to the Dyson School of Applied Economics and Management or the Nolan School of Hotel Administration which are both located in our College of Business.
 Studio classes (architecture, art, design, and fashion) are typically not available to students through general exchange agreements.
 Courses associated with professional graduate degree programs (MBA, MEng, MPS, MPH, etc.) are not available to non-degree students; in general, students should not expect to take graduate (MA, MS, PhD) courses. You will find this information and the additional information in our Fact Sheet.
universities.admission_notes:
Students entering Cornell for a full academic or calendar year are expected to maintain good academic standing. Good academic standing for incoming exchange and visiting students is defined as:

·         Earn a term GPA of 2.0 or higher in their first term at Cornell.

·         Successfully completing a minimum of 12 academic credits. To complete course credits, students must receive a letter grade of “D-” or higher, OR an “S” in a class taken S/U.

Failure to maintain good academic standing for your first term at Cornell will result in dismissal from the program.
